title: Japan trip — running log
body:
## Overview
This is the catch-all log for the two-week autumn trip. Five nights in Tokyo, three in Kyoto, two in Hakone, then back to Tokyo for the flight home. I am keeping everything here so I stop losing details across a dozen apps.

## Tokyo
We are staying in Shinjuku because it is a transit hub and the Romancecar to Hakone leaves from there. The first day is just adjusting to the time difference — a slow walk around the neighborhood, conveyor-belt sushi, and an early night. Day two is teamLab, which has to be booked in advance because same-day tickets sell out by mid-morning. Day three we drift through Shimokitazawa for vintage shops and coffee. The trains are punctual to the minute and the station signage is in English, so getting around is far less stressful than I expected.

## Food notes
The standouts so far: a tiny ramen counter near the hotel with no English menu and a ticket machine at the door, a department-store basement food hall that is overwhelming in the best way, and an izakaya where we pointed at neighboring tables to order. Breakfast is usually a convenience-store rice ball and a canned coffee, which sounds bleak and is somehow excellent.

## Kyoto
The shinkansen to Kyoto is effortless with reserved seats. Kyoto is calmer and older. Fushimi Inari before eight in the morning, before the crowds, is the highlight — the vermilion gates climb the mountain much farther than the photos suggest. Kinkaku-ji is beautiful but packed. We rented bicycles one afternoon and that turned out to be the best way to cover the temples without exhausting ourselves.

## Hakone and practicalities
Hakone is the part that took the most planning. We are doing the loop — pirate ship across the lake, the ropeway over the sulfur vents, the little switchback railway. The ryokan near Gora is the splurge of the trip, with a private open-air bath and a multi-course kaiseki dinner served in the room.

One logistics detail that matters and is easy to forget in the excitement: the ryokan does not take credit cards of any kind, so the entire stay has to be settled in yen on arrival, and there is no ATM within easy walking distance. We are pulling out enough at the airport on the way in to cover the room, dinner, and a buffer, because getting caught short there would be genuinely stressful. Luggage is being forwarded from the Tokyo hotel by the takkyubin service so we only carry an overnight bag on the Hakone leg.

## Heading home
The last night is back in Tokyo near the airport line. The plan is to keep the final day light — a last bowl of ramen, any souvenirs we have been putting off, and an early start for the flight. If I do this again I will spend one fewer day in Tokyo and one more in Kyoto.

## Connectivity and money
We picked up a pocket wifi device at the airport counter on arrival, which has been more reliable than relying on cafe networks and means maps and translation work everywhere. The Suica card loaded onto the phone covers nearly all train and bus travel with a tap, and convenience stores top it up. Most restaurants and shops take cards now, so day to day we carry only a modest amount of cash. The exception is the ryokan, noted above, plus a couple of tiny old-school places that hand you a handwritten bill, so a small cash float is still worth keeping. I am tracking spending in a simple note each evening so the trip total does not become a surprise at the end.

title: Kitchen renovation — notes and decisions
body:
## Goal
Gut and redo the kitchen over roughly eight weeks without moving out. The big moves are new cabinets, a peninsula instead of the wall that boxes the room in, better lighting, and replacing the old coil cooktop. I am logging decisions here so I can answer the contractor quickly.

## Layout
Taking down the half-wall between the kitchen and the dining area opens the whole space and lets us add a peninsula with seating for three. The sink moves under the window, which means re-routing the drain a couple of feet — the plumber says that is straightforward. We are keeping the fridge in its current alcove to avoid moving the water line for the ice maker.

## Cabinets and counters
Shaker fronts, painted a warm off-white, with deep drawers instead of lower shelves because drawers are far easier to actually use. Quartz counters for the low maintenance. A simple ceramic tile backsplash. We went back and forth on open shelving and decided on one short run of it by the window and closed cabinets everywhere else.

## Appliances and electrical
This is where it got complicated. We want an induction range, which everyone who has cooked on one swears by — instant control, a cool surface, easy cleanup. The catch is power. The electrician came out to look at the existing service and was direct about it: the current panel is already near its limit, and an induction range pulls a large dedicated circuit, so before the range can go in at all the house needs a service upgrade to a 200-amp panel. That is its own permit and a day of work, and it has to be scheduled before the cabinets go in because the panel is on the wall we are refacing. Until that upgrade happens, none of the new appliance circuits can be pulled. We are treating it as the first hard dependency of the whole job.

## Lighting
Recessed cans on a dimmer for general light, a pair of pendants over the peninsula, and under-cabinet LED strips for the counters, which the last kitchen badly lacked. All of it on the new circuits, which again waits on the panel.

## Schedule
Rough order: demo, the panel upgrade, rough plumbing and electrical, drywall patch, cabinets, counters templated and installed a week later, backsplash, appliances, then paint and punch list. The counters being templated only after cabinets are set is the other long pole. I am bracing for the timeline to slip by a couple of weeks because it always does.

## Budget and contingency
The cabinets and counters are the biggest line items by far, followed by appliances and then labor. We set aside a contingency of about fifteen percent because old houses always hide something behind the walls, and this one did not disappoint — there was knob-and-tube wiring in one corner that has to be replaced while everything is open, which is unglamorous money but the right time to spend it. The induction range and the panel upgrade together are a meaningful chunk we did not fully price at the start.

## Living through it
Cooking out of a microwave and a hot plate in the dining room for two months is the part nobody warns you about. We set up a temporary sink in the laundry room and have leaned hard on one-pot meals and takeout. The dust gets everywhere despite the plastic sheeting. It is worth it, but I am counting the days until the first real dinner cooked on the new range.

title: Postmortem — checkout outage, May 3
body:
## Summary
For about thirty-seven minutes on the evening of May 3, the checkout service returned errors for most users. No data was lost. This document is the blameless writeup: what happened, why, how we responded, and what we are changing.

## Timeline
The deploy that triggered the incident went out at 1:52am. Alerting fired at 2:06am when checkout error rate crossed the page threshold. The on-call engineer acknowledged within two minutes and pulled in a second responder. There was a brief debate about whether to roll forward with a hotfix or revert. Priya made the call to roll back at 2:14am, judging that a clean revert was lower risk than a fix authored at two in the morning. The rollback completed and error rates returned to normal by 2:29am.

## What happened
The May 3 deploy included a change that doubled the number of background workers processing post-checkout tasks. Each worker opens its own database connection. The intent was to clear a growing queue faster.

## Root cause
The underlying cause was not the application code itself but the database connection budget. When the worker count doubled, the total number of connections requested jumped past what the database was configured to allow, and Postgres began refusing new connections. Because the checkout path and the background workers draw from the same pool, the user-facing checkout requests started losing the race for a connection and erroring out. In other words, the connection pool was exhausted once the deploy doubled the workers, and the customer traffic was starved as a side effect of a background change. It was a capacity-planning miss, not a logic bug.

## Detection and response
Detection was fast — fourteen minutes from deploy to page — which is roughly where we want it. The response was clean. The one rough edge was the few minutes spent debating roll-forward versus rollback; a clearer default ("when in doubt at night, revert") would have saved them.

## Action items
First, add an alarm on connection-pool utilization so we see the ceiling approaching instead of hitting it. Second, cap the background worker concurrency and make it a reviewed configuration value rather than something a feature change can quietly double. Third, load-test the worker fan-out before the next change to it. Fourth, write the "revert by default overnight" guidance into the on-call runbook so the next person does not have to make the call cold.

## Customer impact
During the window, most checkout attempts failed with a generic error and a retry prompt. We estimate a few thousand abandoned carts, though a good share retried successfully after recovery. Support saw a spike in tickets that tapered within the hour. No payments were double-charged and no orders were lost, because the failures happened before the charge step, which is the one piece of good luck in the whole event.

## What went well and what did not
What went well: detection was fast, the responders worked calmly, and the rollback was clean and quick. What did not: the change that doubled the workers shipped without anyone modeling its effect on the shared connection budget, and the overnight roll-forward-versus-revert debate cost a few minutes we did not need to spend. The deeper lesson is that a background change quietly competing for the same finite resource as the user-facing path is a class of failure we should design against, not just alert on.

title: Annual physical — visit notes
body:
## Before the visit
Booked the yearly checkup, fasted the morning for bloodwork, and wrote down the few things I wanted to ask about: occasional afternoon fatigue, a nagging right shoulder, and whether I actually need to keep taking the over-the-counter stuff in the cabinet.

## The exam
Blood pressure was fine, slightly better than last year, which the doctor credited to the running. Heart and lungs clear. The shoulder is almost certainly a minor rotator-cuff strain from desk posture and bench press form; the plan is a set of band exercises and to come back if it does not settle in six weeks. Weight steady. The general read was that things look good.

## Bloodwork
Most panels came back unremarkable — cholesterol in range, blood sugar normal, kidney and liver markers fine, thyroid normal. The one thing that stood out was a vitamin level. My vitamin D came back low, at twenty-two, which is under the threshold and probably explains some of the winter fatigue. The recommendation was concrete: start two thousand international units of vitamin D daily, take it with a meal that has some fat so it absorbs, and recheck the level in about three months to confirm it has come up before deciding whether to continue through next winter. Everything else needs no action.

## Lifestyle
We talked about sleep, which has been inconsistent, and the doctor reinforced the boring fundamentals — consistent wake time, light in the morning, less screen late. On diet, nothing dramatic; more fish and leafy greens, which dovetails with the vitamin conversation. Keep the running but add one full rest day because the shoulder and a niggling knee both read as overuse rather than injury.

## Follow-ups
Schedule the routine dental and eye checks, which are overdue. Set a reminder for the three-month bloodwork recheck. Start the shoulder band routine this week. And actually take the vitamin instead of letting the bottle sit in the cabinet, which is the honest reason the level is low in the first place.

## Family history and risk
We went over family history again since it shapes what to watch. There is heart disease on one side and type-two diabetes on the other, so the normal cholesterol and blood-sugar numbers this year are reassuring but not a reason to coast. The doctor framed the running, the steady weight, and decent blood pressure as exactly the levers that matter for that family risk, and encouraged keeping them up rather than chasing anything dramatic.

## Questions for next time
A few things to raise at the next visit: whether the occasional afternoon fatigue fully resolves once the vitamin level comes up, whether the shoulder needs imaging if the band exercises do not fix it in six weeks, and whether to start a baseline for anything age-related now rather than later. I am writing these down because I always forget half my questions the moment I am actually in the room, and then kick myself in the parking lot afterward for the one thing I meant to mention and did not.

title: Used car research — the wagon
body:
## What I want
A used wagon, two or three years old, manual if I can find one but I have made peace with an automatic. Reliable over flashy. Good visibility, enough cargo room for bikes and camping gear, and a clean history. I am keeping notes on each candidate here so I stop re-researching the same things.

## The shortlist
Three trims keep coming up. The base is honest and cheap but a little sparse. The mid trim adds the safety package I actually care about — automatic emergency braking and blind-spot monitoring — and is the sweet spot on price. The top trim adds leather and a sunroof I do not need. So the target is a two-year-old mid trim with the safety package, under a sensible mileage ceiling.

## The specific car
There is a 2021 mid-trim at a dealer across town that ticks the boxes: one owner, service records, no accidents on the history report, and mileage on the low side for its age. I went and drove it and it was quiet and tight, no warning lights, brakes and steering felt right.

## The recall question
The one thing that gave me pause is that this model year had a known issue. There was a transmission recall on the 2021 wagons — a software and valve-body problem that could cause harsh or delayed shifts — and I did not want to inherit that. I ran the VIN through the manufacturer's recall lookup and called the dealer's service desk to confirm. The answer was reassuring: this particular VIN was built after the production fix, in the September batch, and the recall lookup shows no open campaigns against it. So on this specific car the transmission recall is already resolved and not an outstanding risk, which takes my biggest worry off the table.

## Costs
The asking price is a little above average for the mileage, but the clean history and the resolved recall are worth something. I will counter modestly. Insurance quotes came in reasonable. I budgeted for new tires within a year because the current set is about half worn, and for the routine timing-service interval that is coming up.

## Decision
Leaning yes. The plan is one more independent inspection by a mechanic I trust, and if that comes back clean, make the offer. The resolved recall, the single owner, and the service history are what move it from a maybe to a probably.

## Financing and total cost
I am paying mostly cash but financing a small portion to keep a cushion, and the credit-union rate beat the dealer's offer by enough to matter. Beyond the sticker, the real number is total cost of ownership: insurance came in reasonable, the fuel economy is good for a wagon, and the service intervals are not exotic. I budgeted separately for the near-term tires and the upcoming timing service so they do not feel like surprises later.

## Alternatives considered
I looked hard at a crossover instead, which has more ground clearance and a higher seating position, but the wagon drives better, parks easier, and swallows the same gear with the seats down. I also considered buying a year newer to dodge the recall-year entirely, but the price jump was steep and, with this specific VIN's recall already resolved, the older year is the better value. The wagon it is, pending the inspection.

title: Carrying cash while traveling
body: Notes to self on money abroad. Pull cash from a bank ATM rather than an airport exchange counter — the rate is better and the fees are lower. Tell the bank your travel dates so the card is not frozen on the first withdrawal. Carry a small amount of local currency for the first day before you find an ATM. Split cash between a wallet and a bag so a loss is not catastrophic. Many places are card-first now, but markets, small family restaurants, and some inns are cash-only, so never go to zero. A no-foreign-transaction-fee card is worth it for everything else.

title: Home electrical panel basics
body: A quick primer I wrote after the last project. The panel rating, in amps, is the total the house can draw — older homes are often one hundred amps, many newer ones two hundred. Each breaker protects a circuit sized to its wire. Big appliances — ranges, dryers, EV chargers, AC — want their own dedicated high-amp circuits. If the panel is near its rated load you cannot just add more big circuits; you need a service upgrade first, which is utility coordination, a permit, and an electrician's day. Signs you are close: frequent trips, a panel with no free slots, lights dimming when the dryer starts.

title: Database connection pooling guide
body: Reference notes on pooling. A connection pool reuses a fixed set of database connections instead of opening one per request. Put a pooler like PgBouncer in front in transaction mode for many short-lived clients. Size the pool near the number of database cores; oversizing causes lock contention, not throughput. Remember that every process drawing on the database — web workers, background jobs, cron tasks — shares the same ceiling, so a change in one can starve another. Watch pool utilization as a first-class metric; hitting the connection limit looks like errors, not slowness.

title: Supplements — what actually helps
body: Skeptic's notes on supplements. For most people eating a varied diet, most pills do nothing. The few with reasonable evidence in specific cases: vitamin D if you are deficient or get little sun, B12 for vegans and older adults, iron only if bloodwork shows low, and omega-3s if you rarely eat fish. Everything is dose- and context-dependent, so test before supplementing rather than guessing. Take fat-soluble vitamins with a meal. More is not better; some are harmful in excess. The honest summary: fix sleep, food, and sunlight first, and treat pills as targeted patches for measured gaps.

title: Transmission maintenance basics
body: General notes on keeping a transmission healthy. Change the fluid on the manufacturer's interval — old fluid is the most common cause of harsh shifting on an automatic. Let the car warm up before hard driving in the cold. Come to a full stop before shifting between drive and reverse. Do not ride the brake and accelerator together. Harsh or delayed shifts, slipping, or a burnt smell mean get it looked at promptly. On a used car, a transmission service record and a smooth test drive across the full range of gears tell you most of what you need to know.

title: Docker compose for local dev
body:
## Why
A reproducible local stack without polluting the host.

## Compose file
```yaml
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "pg_isready"]
  app:
    build: .
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
volumes:
  pgdata:
```

## Run
Bring it up detached with docker compose up -d. The app waits for the database because depends_on uses condition service_healthy tied to the db healthcheck, so it will not start until pg_isready passes. The named volume survives a down.
